Maximizing Sales Engineering Performance



Capstone

Hypothesis Testing

CRM Analytics

Python

R

SQL

Salesforce

Gong



MSBA Capstone with one of the nation’s largest PropTech companies — matched 43K Gong conversation recordings to 6,039 Salesforce CRM opportunities to uncover what drives Sales Engineering deal success. Integrated semi-structured call data with structured CRM records, then applied chi-square tests, z-tests, and logistic regression to 5 behavioral hypotheses. Key findings: multi-product SE engagement lifts win rate 34.6% → 67.1% (+32pp); outside-sales SE coverage gap >$50K represents ~$555K untapped opportunity; balanced SE talk ratio (40–55%) predicts wins only in mid-tier $5K–$27K deals. Delivered a win-probability scoring dashboard for SE leadership to prioritize high-opportunity accounts.



May 15, 2026

title: Sun Country Airlines Customer Segmentation
text:
Sun Country Airlines needed a data-driven approach to understand its customer base. A one-size-fits-all marketing strategy fails to capture the diverse motivations of different traveler types — leisure families, frequent business flyers, and budget solo travelers all behave differently and respond to different offers.
Our goal: segment Sun Country’s customer population into meaningful, actionable groups using unsupervised learning, then translate those segments into concrete marketing recommendations.

title: Sun Country Airlines Customer Segmentation
text:
Sun Country Airlines needed a data-driven approach to understand its customer base. A one-size-fits-all marketing strategy fails to capture the diverse motivations of different traveler types — leisure families, frequent business flyers, and budget solo travelers all behave differently and respond to different offers.
Our goal: segment Sun Country’s customer population into meaningful, actionable groups using unsupervised learning, then translate those segments into concrete marketing recommendations.

title: Sun Country Airlines Customer Segmentation
text:
Data

Source: Sun Country Airlines internal trip records
Size: 1.86M trip records, 1.52M unique customers, 90 variables
Coverage: January 2013 – December 2014
Key variables: age_group, group_size, round_trip, total_spend, days_advance_purchase, UflyRewards membership, origin/destination airport pairs

Preprocessing:

Aggregated from trip-level to customer-level (mean per uid)
Filled missing numeric values with column mean; filled binary flags with 0
Excluded non-feature identifiers (uid, PNRLocatorID)
Features pre-normalized to [0, 1] range for K-Means distance calculations

title: Sun Country Airlines Customer Segmentation
text:
Approach

Stage 1 — EDA
Explored distributions across demographic and behavioral variables. Notable patterns: - Majority of customers booked 0–30 days in advance (opportunistic travelers) - Group size heavily skewed right — most customers travel alone or in pairs - UflyRewards membership correlated with higher spend and advance booking behavior - BOS–MSP and DCA–MSP routes dominated the dataset


Stage 2 — K-Means Clustering
Selected K-Means (scikit-learn) for its interpretability and scalability on 1.5M+ records.
Choosing k: Used the elbow method on inertia across k=2 to 10. The curve showed a clear inflection at k=5 — capturing meaningful behavioral variation without over-segmenting.
Model: KMeans(n_clusters=5, random_state=42) on the customer-level aggregated feature matrix.

title: Sun Country Airlines Customer Segmentation
text:
Segments








Cluster
Label
Key Profile




0
Budget Solo Travelers
Low spend, older (35–55+), likely business purpose, fly alone


1
Family Vacationers
Higher group size (avg 1.5), round-trip heavy (0.8), mid-age (35–54), tropical destinations


2
Mature Small-Group Travelers
Higher spend, advance booking, mid-age, Washington D.C. origin


3
Budget Professional Travelers
Low spend, young adults (25–34 / 35–54), BOS↔︎DCA commute + leisure mix


4
Group Budget Travelers
Mature 35+, budget fares, larger groups, beach destinations




Segment Profiles in Detail
Cluster 0 — Budget Solo Travelers Predominantly older travelers flying solo on economy fares. Low UflyRewards engagement. Likely price-sensitive and infrequent flyers who book close to departure.
Cluster 1 — Family Vacationers Higher group sizes suggest family travel. Strong round-trip preference and mid-age demographics point to leisure-motivated families. Destination patterns skew toward warmer vacation markets.
Cluster 2 — Mature Small-Group Travelers Highest average spend per customer. Tend to book further in advance — a signal of planned travel rather than opportunistic booking. Washington D.C. as a common origin suggests professional or government-adjacent travel.
Cluster 3 — Budget Professional Travelers Younger demographic, lower spend, concentrated on the BOS–DCA corridor — consistent with commuter-style travel between Boston and Washington. Mix of business and leisure suggests a “bleisure” segment.
Cluster 4 — Group Budget Travelers Mature travelers flying in groups to beach destinations. Budget-conscious but travel together — responsive to group discount offers and package deals.

title: Sun Country Airlines Customer Segmentation
text:
Business Implications
Targeted marketing by segment:

Family Vacationers (C1): Family package deals, resort partnerships, early-bird family pricing
Professional Travelers (C3): Commuter pass programs, loyalty point accelerators on BOS–DCA routes
Mature Small-Group (C2): Premium upgrade offers, concierge service upsells, advance-purchase discounts
Group Budget (C4): Group booking discounts, bundled hotel + flight deals, beach destination promotions
Budget Solo (C0): Flash sale alerts, last-minute fare notifications

Loyalty program (Ufly Rewards): Clusters 2 and 3 show the highest engagement potential. Personalized tier incentives for these segments could drive retention and revenue per customer.
Product development: The existence of a strong family segment (C1) suggests opportunity in expanding Sun Country’s leisure route network to family-friendly destinations, or partnering with vacation rental platforms.

title: Sun Country Airlines Customer Segmentation
text:
Limitations

Temporal scope: 2013–2014 data may not reflect current travel behavior post-pandemic restructuring
Missing context: No revenue-per-flight or cost data — segments are behavioral, not profitability-ranked
Static snapshot: Customer segments shift over time; real deployment would require periodic re-clustering
External factors: Macroeconomic conditions, fuel costs, and competitor pricing not captured

title: Maximizing Sales Engineering Performance
text:
The Sales Engineering (SE) team at one of the nation’s largest PropTech companies closes deals worth 4× more than deals without SE coverage — yet leadership had no data to prove it. SE contribution was invisible in the CRM: no way to tie SE activity to win rates, deal velocity, or revenue outcomes.
Our mission: quantify SE’s true impact, identify the behavioral drivers of deal success, and deliver a scoring tool SE leadership can use to prioritize where to focus.



Program
UCI Paul Merage MSBA Capstone · Jan 2026 – June 2026


My Role
Analysis Lead

title: Maximizing Sales Engineering Performance
text:
The Sales Engineering (SE) team at one of the nation’s largest PropTech companies closes deals worth 4× more than deals without SE coverage — yet leadership had no data to prove it. SE contribution was invisible in the CRM: no way to tie SE activity to win rates, deal velocity, or revenue outcomes.
Our mission: quantify SE’s true impact, identify the behavioral drivers of deal success, and deliver a scoring tool SE leadership can use to prioritize where to focus.



Program
UCI Paul Merage MSBA Capstone · Jan 2026 – June 2026


My Role
Analysis Lead

title: Maximizing Sales Engineering Performance
text:
Data
Two systems, joined by a single key.








Source
Scale
What It Captures




Salesforce
6,039 opportunities · 61 fields
Deal outcomes, ACV, SE assignment, stage progression


Gong
43,000 calls · 13 tables
Talk ratios, call cadence, conversation engagement


Matched Dataset
802 deals with full coverage
Every deal with both outcome AND behavioral signal




Salesforce tells us what happened. Gong tells us how. OppId is the bridge.

title: Maximizing Sales Engineering Performance
text:
Five Hypotheses Tested

H1 — SE Reshapes Why Deals Are Lost
χ² = 20.99, p = 0.0038 · Reject H₀
SE involvement doesn’t just affect win rates — it changes the composition of losses. Deals with SE are 9 percentage points less likely to end in “Client Unresponsive,” but become more prone to stalling on budget.



Loss Reason
With SE
Without SE
Δ




Budget / No Funding
18.9%
13.0%
+5.9%


Client Unresponsive
8.2%
17.2%
−9.0%


Lost to Competitor
7.0%
4.3%
+2.7%



Recommendation: Before assigning an SE to a complex deal, validate budget availability and decision-making authority. SE time is wasted on deals that will fail for financial reasons a rep could screen out earlier.





Key shifts: Client Unresponsive −9pp, Budget +5.9pp






Full loss reason distribution: With SE vs Without SE







H2 — SE Multiplies Multi-Product Deals
Interaction OR = 3.569, p < 0.0001 · Reject H₀
SE has no measurable effect on single-product deals (p = 0.61) — but nearly doubles the win rate on multi-product deals.



Segment
No SE
With SE
Lift




Single-product
39.3%
37.5%
Not significant


Multi-product
34.6%
67.1%
+32.5 pp



Sweet spot: 4-product bundles carry the highest median ACV ($32K). Beyond 20 products, complexity erodes both win rate and ACV.
Recommendation: Route SE capacity to multi-product opportunities — that’s where SE effort produces measurable ROI.





Win rate: Single vs Multi-product × SE involvement






Win rate by product count bucket × SE involvement







H3 — Talk Ratio: Precision Matters More Than Direction
z = 1.72, p = 0.043 (mid-tier) · Conditional Reject H₀
SEs at this company speak only 29% of call time on average — far below Gong’s 40–55% optimal range. Across all deals, talk ratio shows no significant effect (p = 0.69). But segment by deal size, and a 20-point gap emerges in mid-tier deals.



Deal Tier
Balanced (40–55%)
High Talk (>60%)
Δ




Small (<$5K)
44.1%
55.2%
—


Mid ($5K–$27K)
46.0%
27.3%
+18.7 pp


Large (>$27K)
42.4%
62.5%
—



Recommendation: For mid-complexity deals, coach SEs toward a 40–55% talk ratio. That’s the only tier where the behavior change produces statistically significant, measurable ROI.





Win rate by deal size × talk ratio — significance only in mid-tier






Talk ratio distribution: Won vs Lost







H4 — Outside Sales Win Rate Collapses Above $50K
p = 0.0057 · Reject H₀
Outside sales reps outperform inside sales on small deals — but their win rate halves once deals exceed $50K without technical SE support.



Channel
≤ $50K
> $50K




Inside Sales
42.5%
33.8%


Outside Sales
57.6%
23.4%



SE adds +12.6 percentage points and +$3,019 per deal on outside deals ≥ $50K. Currently 184 of these high-value deals had no SE coverage — representing ~$555K in untapped incremental ACV.
Recommendation: In Salesforce, trigger automatic SE assignment at first touch for Outside Sales + ACV ≥ $50K.



Win rate by SE involvement × sales motion × deal size





H5 — Faster Conversation Cadence Consistently Predicts Winning
Monotonic decline significant · Reject H₀
Win rate falls steadily as the gap between conversations widens — from 63% on deals with calls every 10 days, down to 28% for deals with 80+ day gaps. SE involvement raises the baseline by +10 to +24 percentage points at every cadence level, but the velocity slope is identical whether or not SE is involved (interaction p = 0.173, n.s.).



Avg Gap Between Calls
Win Rate




0–10 days
63% (n = 49)


10–20 days
56% (n = 61)


20–40 days
48% (n = 86)


40–80 days
43% (n = 65)


80+ days
28% (n = 47)



Recommendation: SE cannot compensate for slow follow-up. Regardless of SE coverage, target follow-ups within 0–30 days.

title: Maximizing Sales Engineering Performance
section: Bonus Finding: SE ROI Is Segment-Specific
text:
Bonus Finding: SE ROI Is Segment-Specific
The same ~+28 pp win-rate lift from SE coverage produces dramatically different revenue outcomes across industries.

SE coverage should not be one-size-fits-all: Finance/mid-market delivers 9× the revenue upside per SE hour versus small/university deals.





University segment: +27.5pp win-rate lift, $482K upside per 200 deals






Finance segment: +30.1pp win-rate lift, $4.18M upside per 200 deals

title: Maximizing Sales Engineering Performance
text:
Deliverable: Win-Probability Scoring Tool
We translated all five hypotheses into an interactive Sales Intelligence dashboard — a step-by-step calculator SE leadership can use to score any deal in the pipeline in real time.
Inputs: Product count · SE assigned · Engagement cadence · Sales channel · Talk ratio
Output: Predicted win probability with full math trace — every percentage point traces back to a specific hypothesis finding.

From blind spot to decision — in one view.

title: Smartphone Predictive Pricing Analysis
text:
The global smartphone market generates billions in revenue, yet pricing decisions are surprisingly opaque. Which specs actually move the needle on price — and which are just marketing noise?
Our team tackled two questions:

Which smartphone specifications most significantly influence price?
Can we build an effective model to predict smartphone prices from published specs alone?

title: Smartphone Predictive Pricing Analysis
text:
The global smartphone market generates billions in revenue, yet pricing decisions are surprisingly opaque. Which specs actually move the needle on price — and which are just marketing noise?
Our team tackled two questions:

Which smartphone specifications most significantly influence price?
Can we build an effective model to predict smartphone prices from published specs alone?

title: Smartphone Predictive Pricing Analysis
text:
Data

Source: Kaggle — Real World Smartphones Dataset (Abhijit Dhatonde)
Size: 980 entries × 22 variables; 949 usable after cleaning
Coverage: 46 brands, 13 processor families, prices in Indian Rupees (INR)
Notable: Right-skewed price distribution (mean ≈ 75th percentile); max ₹650,000 (Vertu luxury brand)

Key variables: price, processor_speed, ram_capacity, internal_memory, battery_capacity, resolution_height/width, os (engineered → ios_not)

title: Smartphone Predictive Pricing Analysis
text:
Approach

Stage 1 — EDA & Preprocessing
Price distribution was severely right-skewed (skewness = 12.68), signalling a log transformation would be needed. Correlation heatmap revealed a strong negative relationship between processor_speed and extended_memory_available — high-end phones drop SD card slots in favour of fast internal storage — flagging multicollinearity risk.
Cleaning decisions:

Median imputation for avg_rating, processor_speed, battery_capacity (40+ missing each)
Dropped fast_charging (211 missing), model (980 unique classes), processor_brand (13 classes) — high-cardinality or redundant
Engineered ios_not binary flag to capture Apple’s ecosystem premium



Stage 2 — OLS Baseline
Full-variable OLS revealed the problem: R² = 0.430, condition number = 1.76 × 10⁵ (severe multicollinearity). Multiple features had insignificant p-values. Regularization was needed.


Stage 3 — Lasso Feature Selection
LassoCV (10-fold, α ≈ 1759) compressed 15 features down to 8 meaningful predictors:



Feature
Lasso Coeff
Direction




internal_memory
11,988
positive


ios_not
5,538
positive


resolution_width
4,078
positive


resolution_height
2,607
positive


processor_speed
1,265
positive


extended_memory_available
-6,069
negative


battery_capacity
-1,971
negative


fast_charging_available
-955
negative





Stage 4 — Linear Regression with Lasso Features
Re-fitting OLS on the 8 selected features: R² = 0.730. Residual analysis still showed heteroscedasticity — prediction errors fanned out at higher prices.


Stage 5 — Log-Linear Regression (Primary Model)
Applied log(price + 1) transformation. R² = 0.847 — explains ~85% of price variation. Residuals became approximately normal with heteroscedasticity largely resolved.
Key log-linear coefficients:








Feature
Coefficient
Interpretation




ios_not
+0.584
iOS adds ~79% price premium over Android


processor_speed
+0.454
Each GHz adds ~57% to price


extended_memory_available
-0.279
SD card slot signals lower-tier device


internal_memory
+0.002
Each GB adds ~0.2%





Stage 6 — Random Forest
To capture non-linear interactions (e.g., “small screen + iOS + high performance” = premium), trained Random Forest (100 trees) on Lasso-selected features: R² = 0.823.
Top feature importances: resolution_height (28%), processor_speed (25%), battery_capacity (17%). Notably ios_not dropped to 1.5% — because RF splits on hardware specs that already isolate the iPhone cluster, making OS redundant.

title: Smartphone Predictive Pricing Analysis
text:
Model Comparison








Model
R²
Notes




OLS (all features)
0.430
Severe multicollinearity


LR + Lasso features
0.730
Clean feature set, still heteroscedastic


Log-Linear (primary)
0.847
Best interpretability, satisfies regression assumptions


Random Forest
0.823
Best for non-linear interactions, less interpretable

title: Smartphone Predictive Pricing Analysis
text:
Key Findings
Performance drives price. Processor speed, internal memory, and screen resolution are the most consistent pricing factors across every model — fundamental to perceived product value.
Peripheral features add noise, not price. Fast charging, SD card support, and camera count show weak or negative relationships with price once performance specs are controlled.
The iOS premium is real and large. Even after fully controlling for hardware, iOS devices command a ~79% price premium — capturing Apple’s ecosystem, software longevity, and brand equity.
An interesting model contradiction: Log-linear attributes large influence to ios_not, while Random Forest nearly ignores it. RF first splits on resolution and processor speed, which effectively identifies the “high-end device” cluster (which happens to include most iPhones). Once that cluster is isolated, OS provides little additional information gain — the brand premium is absorbed into hardware splits.

title: Smartphone Predictive Pricing Analysis
text:
Limitations

980 observations is modest for ML — limits generalization to niche brands and new releases
Prices in INR, not USD — market conditions and tariffs limit cross-market applicability
No temporal dimension — snapshot data; cannot capture depreciation curves or launch cycles
Macroeconomic blind spots — supply chain costs, import tariffs, and regional purchasing power are not captured

title: Global Supply Chain Management Simulation
text:
In UCI’s Supply Chain Analytics course, our team of four ran the Harvard Business Publishing Global Supply Chain Management Simulation V2 — a multi-round exercise where we acted as the supply chain leadership of a smartphone manufacturer, making interconnected decisions across product design, demand forecasting, and production sourcing over four simulated years.








Detail




Simulation
HBP Global Supply Chain Management V2


Team
Team 7 — 4 members


Models
Model A (lower markdown cost) · Model B (high liquidation cost)


Decision areas
Product design · Demand forecasting · Supplier selection · Production orders


Total profit
$197,480,640 across 4 years

title: Global Supply Chain Management Simulation
text:
In UCI’s Supply Chain Analytics course, our team of four ran the Harvard Business Publishing Global Supply Chain Management Simulation V2 — a multi-round exercise where we acted as the supply chain leadership of a smartphone manufacturer, making interconnected decisions across product design, demand forecasting, and production sourcing over four simulated years.








Detail




Simulation
HBP Global Supply Chain Management V2


Team
Team 7 — 4 members


Models
Model A (lower markdown cost) · Model B (high liquidation cost)


Decision areas
Product design · Demand forecasting · Supplier selection · Production orders


Total profit
$197,480,640 across 4 years

title: Global Supply Chain Management Simulation
text:
Simulation Structure
Each year consisted of three decision rooms:
Design Room — Choose up to 2 feature options (e.g., Upgraded Communication, Stylish, High Quality Audio). Each option shifts the expected demand distribution for Model A and/or B, affecting both the mean and variance of forecasts.
Forecast Room — Listen to 5 internal forecasters with different expertise levels. Combine their inputs to produce a single demand forecast for each model. Credibility weighting and variance between forecasters both matter.
Production Room — Allocate production across 4 suppliers with different cost/lead-time tradeoffs:



Supplier
Cost
Lead Time
Best Use




FarFarAway
Lowest
4 months
Predictable, high-volume base


FarAway
Moderate
Medium
Mid-flexibility buffer


PrettyClose
Higher
Short
Reactive adjustments


VeryClose
Highest
Shortest
Emergency only



A key asymmetry: Model B carries much higher holding, markdown, and liquidation costs than Model A — any overstock of Model B is disproportionately expensive.

title: Global Supply Chain Management Simulation
text:
Year-by-Year Strategy

Year 1 — Establishing a Baseline
Design: Upgraded Communication + Stylish. Rationale: align with the company’s “faster and sleeker” design brief. Stylish partially offset the demand variance introduced by Upgraded Communication.
Forecast: Selectively weighted — trusted Andrei (industry experience, Y) and Claire (specialist forecaster, Y) over less-credentialed forecasters. Model A: 59.5k, Model B: 35k.
Suppliers: FarFarAway (50k Model A, 10k Model B) + FarAway (10k Model B) + PrettyClose (reactive buffer for Model B). Issued one production change order mid-year after Model B demand increased, adjusting PrettyClose upward.
Result: $47,327,100 | Board votes: 2/5 Board criticism centered on the communication feature choice and limited supplier flexibility.



Year 2 — Shift Toward Predictability
Design: Stylish + Storage Capacity. Both options reduced standard deviation across both models. Avoided Extended Battery and Durability specifically because they introduced extreme demand variance — the priority shifted from demand mean to demand predictability.
Forecast: Used the average of all forecasters given low inter-forecaster variance. Model A: 55k, Model B: 32k.
Suppliers: Same structure as Year 1. Issued two production change orders after major demand events caused sharp drops — reduced FarFarAway and FarAway production to avoid excess Model B inventory.
Result: $51,572,640 | Board votes: 3/5 Improvement recognized in design option evaluation; feedback noted need for better sourcing flexibility.



Year 3 — Leaner and More Flexible
Design: High Quality Audio only. This was the only option that decreased variance for both models while also lifting Model B’s average expected demand. Rejected Extended Battery, Durability, and Anti-Theft — all increased demand uncertainty significantly.
Forecast: Model A: 67k (above average of 65; holding cost is low so slight overstock is acceptable). Model B: 26k (below average of 29; liquidation cost risk justified a conservative stance).
Suppliers: Dropped FarAway entirely — $2M setup cost, and over-reliance in Year 2 had reduced flexibility. Shifted to FarFarAway (60k Model A) + PrettyClose (26k Model B + 7k Model A buffer). Issued 2 change orders to reduce PrettyClose production as Model B demand fell.
Result: $44,326,300 | Board votes: 4/5 Lower profit due to ongoing demand volatility in Model B, but board recognized improved inventory balancing and design option reasoning.



Year 4 — Optimized and Disciplined
Design: High Quality Audio again. Achieved SD = 1 for Model A — near-perfect predictability. Only slight increase in Model B variance in exchange for higher average demand on both models.
Forecast: Model A: 63k (simple average, SD = 1 makes it reliable). Model B: 20k (cautious — historical demand consistently underperformed; average was 26k but SD = 9).
Suppliers: FarFarAway (60k Model A) + PrettyClose (20k Model B + 3k Model A). Did not issue production change orders this year.
Result: $54,254,600 | Board votes: 4/5 Highest single-year profit. Board recognized understanding of forecast credibility weighting and the asymmetric cost structures of Model A vs. B.

title: Global Supply Chain Management Simulation
text:
Results Summary



Year
Net Profit
Board Votes




Year 1
$47,327,100
2 / 5


Year 2
$51,572,640
3 / 5


Year 3
$44,326,300
4 / 5


Year 4
$54,254,600
4 / 5


Total
$197,480,640
—



Board vote improvement from 2/5 → 4/5 reflects the strategy’s maturation: better design option evaluation, more disciplined supplier selection, and more context-sensitive inventory decisions.

title: Global Supply Chain Management Simulation
text:
Key Lessons
Demand variance matters as much as demand level. Choosing design options that lower the standard deviation — even at the cost of a slightly lower expected mean — improved forecast accuracy and inventory outcomes more than chasing higher average demand with higher uncertainty.
Asymmetric inventory costs require asymmetric strategies. Model B’s high liquidation cost meant that the optimal forecast was below the mean (intentionally conservative), while Model A’s lower markdown cost allowed slight overstock. The same “average-based” rule would have been appropriate for one model and costly for the other.
Cheapest supplier ≠ best supplier. FarFarAway’s low cost was valuable for predictable Model A production, but its 4-month lead time made it unsuitable for the reactive Model B supply. FarAway was dropped in Year 3 because its $2M setup cost couldn’t be justified when PrettyClose provided equivalent flexibility at lower overhead.
Forecast credibility weighting outperforms consensus. Early rounds demonstrated that averaging all forecasters diluted signal from high-credibility sources. Weighting by track record and expertise — particularly Andrei’s industry experience — improved accuracy.
Production change orders are a tool, not a fallback. Issuing 2 change orders in Years 2 and 3 to reduce overexposed Model B inventory reflected proactive demand monitoring, not reactive panic. Year 4’s zero change orders reflected supply alignment with demand — a sign of improved forecasting, not passivity.

title: Predicting Bank Marketing Campaign Subscriptions
text:
A Portuguese bank ran telemarketing campaigns from 2008–2010, calling customers to promote term deposit subscriptions. Only ~11% of customers said yes — the vast majority declined. The business problem: how do you predict before picking up the phone which customers are actually worth calling?
Wasted calls mean wasted time, labor, and budget. A good model translates directly into campaign ROI.

title: Predicting Bank Marketing Campaign Subscriptions
text:
A Portuguese bank ran telemarketing campaigns from 2008–2010, calling customers to promote term deposit subscriptions. Only ~11% of customers said yes — the vast majority declined. The business problem: how do you predict before picking up the phone which customers are actually worth calling?
Wasted calls mean wasted time, labor, and budget. A good model translates directly into campaign ROI.

title: Predicting Bank Marketing Campaign Subscriptions
text:
Data

Source: UCI Bank Marketing Dataset — Portuguese bank, 41,188 records, May 2008–November 2010
Size after preprocessing: 43,193 rows × 13 features
Target: y — did the customer subscribe to a term deposit? (binary; ~11% positive — severe class imbalance)








Feature Group
Variables




Demographics
age, job (employed/retired/unemployed), marital status, education level


Financial
avg. bank balance, housing loan, personal loan, credit default


Campaign
call duration, # contacts this campaign, days since last contact, # previous contacts

title: Predicting Bank Marketing Campaign Subscriptions
text:
Approach
Tools: Python · scikit-learn · imbalanced-learn · pandas · matplotlib · seaborn (Google Colab)

Preprocessing

Removed rows with “unknown” entries in job, marital, education
Simplified 12 job categories → 3: employed / retired / unemployed
Dropped low-signal columns: contact type, day, month, previous outcome
One-hot encoded categorical variables; converted yes/no → 1/0
Removed duration (call length only known after the call — a leakage feature)



Models

Decision Tree Classifier — captures non-linear relationships, produces interpretable splits
Logistic Regression — linear baseline with interpretable coefficients



Experiments
Each model was evaluated across four pipeline stages:







Stage
Description




Benchmark 1
Numerical features only, minimal preprocessing


Benchmark 2
Full cleaning: binning, encoding, column removal


SMOTE
Synthetic oversampling to balance the 11/89 class split


SMOTE + GridSearchCV
Hyperparameter tuning to maximize Class 1 recall



Primary metric: F1-score (appropriate for imbalanced binary classification). All models evaluated with 10-fold cross-validation.

title: Predicting Bank Marketing Campaign Subscriptions
text:
Findings

Model Comparison (Test F1 Score)









Model
CV F1
Test F1
Notes




Untuned Decision Tree
0.392
0.403
Baseline


Untuned Logistic Regression
0.307
0.316
Baseline


Decision Tree (no duration)
0.242
0.244
Realistic pre-call scenario


Logistic Regression (no duration)
0.018
0.023
Collapsed without leakage


Decision Tree + SMOTE
0.837
0.228
Severe overfitting


Logistic Regression + SMOTE
0.785
0.244
Moderate gain


Tuned Decision Tree
0.471
0.470
Best generalization


Tuned Logistic Regression
0.350
0.330
Modest improvement





Key Takeaways
Data leakage was the biggest trap. duration (call length) was the strongest predictor across all models — but it is only known after the call ends, making it useless for pre-call targeting. Removing it cut F1 from ~0.40 to ~0.24, revealing the true predictive ceiling of the remaining features.
SMOTE overfit badly. Synthetic oversampling pushed cross-validation F1 to 0.84 but collapsed on the test set (0.23). Synthetic minority samples did not generalize to real customer behavior — a cautionary result about evaluating CV scores on SMOTE-resampled training data.
Decision Tree consistently outperformed Logistic Regression on F1 metrics. Non-linear decision boundaries matter here; subscription behavior does not separate cleanly on a hyperplane.
Best model: Tuned Decision Tree (max_depth=5, class_weight='balanced', min_samples_split=10) — Test F1 of 0.47, precision 0.33, recall 0.81. High recall means most real subscribers are captured; lower precision means some non-subscribers are contacted unnecessarily, which is the acceptable trade-off in a marketing context.


Top Predictors (after removing duration)

Campaign contact frequency (more contacts → lower subscription probability)
Housing loan status
Previous campaign outcomes
Job type (retired and unemployed had higher subscription rates)
Education level (tertiary education correlated with higher subscription)

title: Predicting Bank Marketing Campaign Subscriptions
text:
Business Recommendations

Cap contacts at 1–2 per customer. Repeated calling reliably reduces subscription probability. Stop after two attempts.
Prioritize high-signal segments: retired customers, higher education, no personal loans, positive prior campaign history.
Tailor messaging by segment: retired customers respond to security and stability; highly-educated customers respond to long-term growth projections.
Integrate the tuned Decision Tree as a lead scorer. The interpretable split rules can be embedded directly in a campaign management workflow to rank leads before dialing.

title: Predicting Bank Marketing Campaign Subscriptions
text:
Reflections
The leakage discovery was the most important moment of this project. A model with duration looks strong (F1 ~0.40 baseline); without it, the real ceiling becomes clear and the work becomes honest. In a real deployment, this distinction determines whether the model is actually useful.
SMOTE served as a useful negative experiment. The proper approach is to apply SMOTE inside cross-validation folds using a Pipeline, not before splitting — that architectural mistake explained the wide CV/test divergence.
What I would do differently:

Use imblearn.pipeline.Pipeline to apply SMOTE inside CV folds properly
Test ensemble methods — Random Forest and XGBoost handle class imbalance more robustly
Use precision-recall curves instead of a fixed 0.5 threshold, letting business requirements set the operating point


Dataset: UCI Bank Marketing
Code: Google Colab Notebook

title: High Note Freemium Conversion Analysis
text:
High Note, a music streaming platform operating on a freemium model, had 1.2M+ users but only ~3% premium subscriber rate. Ad revenue alone couldn’t cover operating costs, making premium conversion the company’s central business challenge.
Our team analyzed user-level data to answer three questions:

What behavioral and social factors predict premium adoption?
Does having premium-subscriber friends causally increase a user’s conversion probability?
Which variables are most predictive — and how few do you need for accurate targeting?

title: High Note Freemium Conversion Analysis
text:
High Note, a music streaming platform operating on a freemium model, had 1.2M+ users but only ~3% premium subscriber rate. Ad revenue alone couldn’t cover operating costs, making premium conversion the company’s central business challenge.
Our team analyzed user-level data to answer three questions:

What behavioral and social factors predict premium adoption?
Does having premium-subscriber friends causally increase a user’s conversion probability?
Which variables are most predictive — and how few do you need for accurate targeting?

title: High Note Freemium Conversion Analysis
text:
Data

Dataset: 43,827 High Note users (instructor case, University of Minnesota SOBACO lab)
Variables: Demographics (age, gender, country), social network (friend count, subscriber friend count, friend diversity), engagement (songs listened, loved tracks, playlists, shouts, posts), tenure
Target: adopter — binary flag, 1 = upgraded to premium subscription

title: High Note Freemium Conversion Analysis
text:
Approach

Stage 1 — Descriptive Statistics & Adopter vs. Non-Adopter Comparison
Computed means, standard deviations, and ranges for all key variables. Compared adopters (premium) against non-adopters (freemium) across three dimensions:
Demographics: Adopters tend to be slightly younger (avg 26 vs 24), slightly more male (76% vs 66%), and slightly more likely to be from outside the US/UK/Germany. Differences are modest — demographics alone are weak predictors.
Peer Influence: Adopters have an adopter-friend ratio of 0.0496 vs 0.02095 for non-adopters — more than twice the proportion of premium friends. Strong association, but correlation, not causation.
User Engagement: Adopters average 264.3 loved tracks vs 86.8 for non-adopters; 99.4 shouts vs 30. Songs listened: 33,758 vs 17,589. Playlist creation and tenure show smaller differences.



Demographics: adopters skew younger, more male, and more international





Peer influence: adopters have 2× the premium friend ratio of non-adopters





User engagement: adopters average 3× more loved tracks and shouts than non-adopters




Stage 2 — Propensity Score Matching (PSM)
Why PSM? Directly comparing users with vs without premium friends conflates the peer effect with underlying activity levels — highly active users both have more friends AND are more likely to pay. PSM controls for confounders to isolate the causal effect.
Method: - Treatment group: users with ≥1 paying friend (9,823 users) - Control group: users with no paying friends - Covariates: 13 features including age, gender, friend count, songs listened, posts, playlists, tenure - Matching: 1:1 nearest-neighbor on logistic propensity scores
Results:



Metric
Value




Treatment group conversion rate
17.75%


Control group conversion rate
9.76%


Average Treatment Effect (ATE)
+7.99 pp


T-statistic
16.37


P-value
8 × 10⁻⁶⁰



Interpretation: Having at least one paying friend increases a free user’s conversion probability by ~8 percentage points — nearly doubling the baseline rate. The result is highly significant, providing causal evidence that peer influence drives monetization.


Stage 3 — Logistic Regression
Binomial logistic regression to predict adoption and estimate log-odds ratios for each predictor.
Significant at α = 0.00 (11 variables): - Loved tracks, songs listened, playlists, posts - Friend count, subscriber friend count, friend country count, average friend age - Age, tenure, country (good country flag)
Not significant: whether friends are male, shouts
Key insight: Music interaction behaviors (liking tracks, making playlists) and social network diversity (friends from different countries) are the strongest signals. Users who actively engage with platform features AND have geographically diverse networks are most likely to convert.


Stage 4 — Forward Subset Regression
Identified the minimum feature set that captures most predictive power. The accuracy curve plateaus meaningfully at 7 features — these are the minimum needed for consistent targeting accuracy.
Top 7 predictors (in order): 1. Loved tracks 2. Songs listened 3. Subscriber friend count 4. Age 5. Good country 6. Friend country count 7. Friend count



Forward subset regression: R² vs number of predictors

title: High Note Freemium Conversion Analysis
text:
Key Findings
Engagement beats demographics. Loved tracks and songs listened are the top two predictors. How users interact with content matters far more than who they are.
Peer influence is causal, not just correlated. PSM confirms an 8 pp lift from having premium friends after controlling for all activity confounders. This is actionable: seeding premium users into social clusters of free users can drive conversions.
Social network diversity is a hidden signal. Users with friends from more countries are significantly more likely to convert — possibly reflecting early-adopter personality traits or exposure to broader platform culture.
You need only 7 variables. A lean targeting model using just 7 features captures most of the predictive signal — useful for real-time campaign scoring where full data isn’t always available.

title: High Note Freemium Conversion Analysis
text:
Business Recommendations
Referral program: Incentivize existing premium subscribers to refer free-user friends (e.g., one free premium month for both parties on successful conversion). PSM proves their influence is real — turn them into the platform’s conversion engine.
Targeted promotions: Free users who already have premium friends in their social circle have an 8 pp higher baseline conversion potential. Prioritize them for discount campaigns — highest ROI per marketing dollar.
Encourage engagement before conversion asks: Users who loved tracks, created playlists, and shout back-and-forth are far more likely to pay. Nudge new users toward these behaviors before showing upgrade prompts.

title: High Note Freemium Conversion Analysis
text:
Limitations

Observational data — PSM addresses confounding but cannot rule out all unmeasured variables
Single time-period snapshot — no longitudinal tracking of conversion timing
Dataset is anonymized teaching case; real deployment would require A/B testing to validate PSM causal estimates

title: About Me
text:
I’m Jinrui (Jerry) Zhang, a graduate student in Business Analytics at UC Irvine. I’m interested in turning data into actionable business insights through machine learning, statistical modeling, and data visualization.
Skills: Python · R · SQL · scikit-learn · pandas · Tableau · Power BI
Interests: Predictive modeling · Customer analytics · Business intelligence · A/B testing
